description:
You are a database administrator who wants to spot-check long–running SQL statements that were captured by the PostgreSQL slow-query logger.

    1. A CSV file already exists at  
       /home/user/slow_queries.csv

    2. The CSV file is UTF-8 encoded and uses a single header line followed by data lines.  
       Exact, comma-separated content (no extra spaces) already present in the file:

       query_id,db,username,duration_ms,sql_text
       1,inventory,alice,350,SELECT * FROM products;
       2,inventory,bob,1200,UPDATE products SET price = price*1.1 WHERE category='gadgets';
       3,hr,carol,900,SELECT * FROM employees WHERE salary > 70000;
       4,sales,dave,2400,DELETE FROM orders WHERE order_date < '2022-01-01';
       5,analytics,erin,1500,SELECT COUNT(*) FROM page_views WHERE view_date = CURRENT_DATE;

       (There is a Unix newline character, “\n”, at the end of every line above.)

    3. Task – in a single command, filter the rows whose duration_ms value is strictly greater than 1000 and produce a JSON-formatted report that satisfies ALL of the following rules:
       • Output path: /home/user/slow_query_report.json  
       • The JSON must be a single top-level array (no surrounding whitespace) of objects.  
       • Each object must contain EXACTLY these three keys, in this order:
         1) "query_id" (integer)  
         2) "duration_ms" (integer)  
         3) "sql_text" (string, preserved exactly as in the CSV)  
       • The objects must be sorted in descending order by duration_ms.  
       • The final byte of the file must be the newline character “\n”.

       A correct file therefore looks exactly like this (line breaks shown explicitly):

       [
         {
           "query_id": 4,
           "duration_ms": 2400,
           "sql_text": "DELETE FROM orders WHERE order_date < '2022-01-01';"
         },
         {
           "query_id": 5,
           "duration_ms": 1500,
           "sql_text": "SELECT COUNT(*) FROM page_views WHERE view_date = CURRENT_DATE;"
         },
         {
           "query_id": 2,
           "duration_ms": 1200,
           "sql_text": "UPDATE products SET price = price*1.1 WHERE category='gadgets';"
         }
       ]

       (And then a single trailing “\n”.)

    4. Verification log – after creating the JSON report, append a one-line log entry to /home/user/optimization_task.log.  
       • If the file does not yet exist, create it.  
       • Append (or write) EXACTLY the text below followed by a newline:

         slow_query_report.json generated successfully

       The test harness checks for that exact string and the newline terminator.

    5. You do NOT have root privileges. Work only inside /home/user.  
       Hint: tools such as awk, csvkit, jq, or pure shell are available—use whatever lets you complete the job **in one single shell command**.

    Deliverables the grader will look for:
       • /home/user/slow_query_report.json with the content and format described in step 3.  
       • /home/user/optimization_task.log containing the exact line from step 4.

    Once both files have been produced exactly as specified, the task is complete.
name: task_000000_dea1afb1
